county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Records well-preserved from early territorial period.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1850 as one of the original Utah Territory counties. Parowan was the first settlement in southern Utah (1851). Cedar City founded 1851 for iron mining. Iron County was the parent to Beaver, Garfield, Kane, and Washington counties. The Mountain Meadows Massacre occurred in 1857 in what was then Iron County territory.
county_metadata.research_tips: Iron County Recorder in Parowan holds some of the oldest records in southern Utah, from 1851. LDS ward records for Parowan (1851) and Cedar City (1851) are the oldest in southern Utah and essential for genealogical research. The Frontier Homestead State Park in Cedar City has pioneer artifacts and family records. Southern Utah University (Cedar City) Special Collections hold iron mission journals and pioneer diaries. The Mountain Meadows Massacre (1857) generated extensive church, military, and federal investigation records — see Juanita Brooks' research and records at the Utah State Archives. Iron County was the staging point for the Mormon colonization of southern Utah. Cedar City's iron foundry records document early industrial workers. Parowan served as the early county seat of a vast territory extending to the Colorado River. SUU has digitized important pioneer correspondence.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books, deeds of trust, and mortgages from 1850 to present. County Recorder maintains index books.

Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints ward and branch records for Iron County communities. Includes blessing records (births), baptisms, confirmations, ordinations, marriages, membership records, and death notices. Functions as de facto vital records for most of Utah's history.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1850. State registration from 1887 (territorial), comprehensive from 1905.

Historical newspapers from Iron County communities. Utah Digital Newspapers (J. Willard Marriott Library, U of U) has extensive digitized Utah papers. Chronicling America has additional titles.

Cemetery inscriptions, burial records, and sexton records for Iron County. BillionGraves (headquartered in Utah) has particularly strong coverage of Utah cemeteries. DAR and DUP transcriptions also available.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Records intact from early territorial period.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1852. Juab County was settled by Mormon pioneers in 1851 along the route south from Salt Lake City. Nephi was originally called Salt Creek. The Tintic Mining District in eastern Juab County was one of Utah's richest, producing silver, gold, lead, and copper from the 1870s to 1950s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Juab County Recorder in Nephi has deed records from 1852. LDS ward records for Nephi (Salt Creek Ward, organized 1851) document the earliest settlers. The Tintic Mining District (Eureka, Silver City, Mammoth) was one of Utah's most productive — mining claim records at BLM, company employment rolls, and mine inspector reports at the Utah State Archives identify miners from diverse backgrounds including Cornish, Irish, Italian, and Finnish immigrants. The Tintic Mining Museum in Eureka has photographs and family records. Many Tintic miners were non-Mormon, making civil and fraternal organization records more important than church records for this population. Eureka had a significant Slavic community. Check Iron County for pre-1852 records of the southern route. Juab County DUP museum in Nephi has pioneer family files.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1864 from Washington County. Named for Thomas L. Kane, a friend to the Mormons. Kane County includes parts of Grand Staircase-Escalante National Monument and Glen Canyon NRA. The area was settled by Mormon colonists in the 1860s-1870s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Kane County Recorder in Kanab has deed records from 1864. LDS ward records for Kanab (organized 1870), Orderville (1875), and Glendale are the primary genealogical sources. Orderville was a United Order communal experiment (1875-1885) — communal records document every family member and their contributions. Kanab became 'Little Hollywood' — movie location records document temporary residents. Glen Canyon Dam (1966) submerged historic sites; Bureau of Reclamation relocation records at NARA Denver. Check Washington County for pre-1864 records. The Kanab Heritage House and Heritage Museum have pioneer family files. John Wesley Powell expedition records (1869, 1871-72) at the Smithsonian document early exploration. Kane County's polygamy-era families are documented in court records from the 1880s anti-polygamy raids. The Paiute Indian Tribe of Utah has members in Kane County; BIA records at NARA Denver.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Some early territorial capital records transferred to Salt Lake City.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1851. Fillmore was Utah's first territorial capital (1851-1858). Named for President Millard Fillmore. Millard County was one of the earliest organized counties in the territory. The Topaz War Relocation Center (1942-1945) was located in Millard County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Millard County Recorder in Fillmore has deed records from 1851. Fillmore was Utah's first territorial capital — early territorial government records are at the Utah State Archives in Salt Lake City. LDS ward records for Fillmore (organized 1851), Delta, Hinckley, and Oak City on FamilySearch. The Topaz War Relocation Center (1942-1945) interned over 11,000 Japanese Americans — records at NARA (RG 210) are essential for Japanese American genealogy. The Topaz Museum in Delta preserves camp records and family stories. The Territorial Statehouse State Park Museum in Fillmore has pioneer-era documents. Check for early Mormon settlement records tied to the territorial capital period. Millard County saw Black Hawk War skirmishes (1860s) — militia records at Utah State Archives. The Great Basin Experimental Station records at NARA document agricultural families.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Records well-preserved from formation.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1862 from Summit and Weber counties. Morgan County occupies the upper Weber River valley. Settled by Mormon pioneers in the early 1860s. The area was on the route of the transcontinental railroad.
county_metadata.research_tips: Morgan County Recorder in Morgan has deed records from 1862. LDS ward records for Morgan, Mountain Green, Porterville, and Enterprise Wards on FamilySearch are the primary genealogical sources. Morgan County is one of Utah's smallest counties and had a predominantly LDS population through most of its history. The transcontinental railroad (1869) passed through Weber Canyon — UP construction records identify workers. Devil's Slide and the surrounding area attracted a few non-Mormon ranchers whose records differ from the LDS community. The Morgan County News (1908+) covers local families extensively. Check Summit and Weber counties for pre-1862 family records. The Morgan County DUP Museum has pioneer journals and family files. County records are relatively complete from formation because the county avoided fires and major disruptions.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Records sparse due to consistently small population.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1865 from Beaver County. Named for the Paiute people. Piute County is one of Utah's least populated counties. Settled by Mormon colonists in the early 1860s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Piute County Recorder in Junction has deed records from 1865. With a population consistently under 2,000, Piute County's records are thin but complete. LDS ward records for Junction, Circleville, Kingston, and Marysvale are the best genealogical sources — virtually all early settlers were Mormon. Marysvale had a small gold mining district in the 1890s-1900s that attracted a few non-Mormon miners; mining claim records at BLM. Piute County experienced Black Hawk War hostilities in the 1860s — territorial militia records at Utah State Archives name participants. Check Beaver County for pre-1865 family records. The DUP museum in Junction has pioneer accounts. Piute Reservoir construction records at the state engineer's office document water rights holders. Circleville Massacre (1866) records at the Utah State Archives document the tragic event involving Paiute people.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Records sparse due to small and remote population.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1864 from Cache County. Named for Charles C. Rich, an LDS apostle. Rich County occupies the Bear Lake area in northeastern Utah. Settlement began in the early 1860s.
county_metadata.research_tips: Rich County Recorder in Randolph has deed records from 1864. LDS ward records for Randolph, Laketown, Woodruff, and Garden City on FamilySearch are the essential genealogical sources — Charles C. Rich personally directed settlement of the area. Bear Lake attracted settlers from both the Utah and Idaho sides; cross-reference with Bear Lake County, Idaho records for families who moved between the two. Rich County has been consistently one of Utah's least populated; the small population means most families are well-documented in local church and newspaper records. The Oregon Short Line Railroad reached the area in the 1890s. Bear Lake Raspberry Days festival records (early 1900s+) document community families. Check Cache County for pre-1864 records. Randolph served as a livestock shipping point; stock brand records at the Utah State Archives. Rich County had several cooperative dairy herds whose records document participating families.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Exceptional record preservation — home of both state archives and FamilySearch headquarters.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1850 as one of the original Utah Territory counties. Salt Lake City founded by Brigham Young in 1847. State capital, headquarters of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, and home to the Family History Library — the world's largest genealogical library. Bingham Canyon copper mine was one of the world's largest open-pit mines.
county_metadata.research_tips: Salt Lake County Recorder has deed records from 1847 — the oldest in the state, predating territorial organization. The Family History Library (FHL) in Salt Lake City is the world's largest genealogical library with billions of records from 100+ countries. LDS Church History Library holds ward records, membership records, patriarchal blessings, and emigration records — critical for Utah genealogy. The Utah State Archives and Records Service in Salt Lake City holds state and county government records. The Bingham Canyon copper mine (Kennecott) employed thousands of immigrants — company records document Greek, Italian, Japanese, Serbian, Croatian, and Finnish workers in mining communities like Bingham, Copperton, and Magna. The University of Utah's Marriott Library Special Collections hold pioneer journals and mining company records. Fort Douglas military records at NARA. Japantown and Greek Town records document early ethnic communities destroyed by urban renewal.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books, deeds of trust, and mortgages from 1850 to present. County Recorder maintains index books.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1880 from Kane County. San Juan County is Utah's largest county by area. The Hole-in-the-Rock expedition (1879-80) brought Mormon settlers to Bluff. The county includes significant Navajo Nation and Ute Mountain Ute tribal lands.
county_metadata.research_tips: San Juan County Recorder in Monticello holds deed records from 1880. The Hole-in-the-Rock expedition (1879-80) is extensively documented in LDS Church records, pioneer journals, and at the Bluff Fort Historic Site — journals name every family in the expedition. Navajo Nation chapter house records and BIA records at NARA (Navajo Agency) are essential for the large Navajo population. Ute Mountain Ute tribal records through BIA at NARA Denver. LDS ward records for Bluff, Monticello, and Blanding on FamilySearch. The Edge of the Cedars State Park Museum in Blanding has local history collections. San Juan County's uranium boom (1950s-60s) brought prospectors — AEC records at NARA and mining claims at BLM. The San Juan Record newspaper (1916+) covers local families. Check Kane County for pre-1880 territorial records. The county's vast size means communities were isolated — each settlement has distinct records.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Excellent record preservation from earliest settlement.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1852. Sanpete County was settled by Mormon colonists in 1849 under Isaac Morley. Manti was one of the earliest settlements south of Salt Lake Valley. Sanpete County was heavily settled by Scandinavian converts. The Manti Temple (1888) is one of the oldest LDS temples.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sanpete County Recorder in Manti has deed records from 1852. Sanpete County has some of the richest genealogical resources in rural Utah. LDS ward records for Manti (organized 1849), Ephraim, Spring City, Mt. Pleasant, and other communities are extensive on FamilySearch. Sanpete Valley was heavily settled by Danish, Swedish, and Norwegian Mormon converts — cross-reference Scandinavian parish records with LDS emigration ship manifests and Perpetual Emigrating Fund records. The Manti Temple (1888) records on FamilySearch document temple ordinances. Ephraim's Snow College has local history collections. The Sanpete County Heritage book is an essential resource covering pioneer families. Spring City's historic architecture documents Scandinavian building traditions. Black Hawk War (1865-1872) had major engagements in Sanpete County — militia muster rolls and incident reports at the Utah State Archives name participants and casualties.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Sanpete County Heritage, 1847-Present (Daughters of Utah Pioneers, Sanpete County, 1997)
